questionText: The lengths, $X$ cm, of cucumbers from a greenhouse are modelled by $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. It is found that $10\%$ are shorter than $30$ cm and $20\%$ are longer than $42$ cm. (a) Find $\mu$ and $\sigma$ to $3$ significant figures. (b) Cucumbers between $33$ cm and $40$ cm are graded ‘Class 1’. Find the proportion of cucumbers that are Class 1.

steps.description: State the model and the two given facts
steps.explanation: Lengths are normal with unknown $\mu$ and $\sigma$. ‘$10\%$ shorter than $30$’ gives $P(X<30)=0.10$ and ‘$20\%$ longer than $42$’ gives $P(X>42)=0.20$.
Standardising $30$ with $Z=\dfrac{X-\mu}{\sigma}$ keeps the lower-tail area at $0.10$.
With $10\%$ of the area below it, this point lies to the left of the mean, so the standardised value is negative.
Equating the standardised value of $30$ with $z_1$ gives the first equation.

steps.description: Convert and standardise the second condition
steps.explanation: Since $20\%$ exceed $42$, exactly $80\%$ fall below it; converting to the lower tail lets us read $z_2 = 0.8416$.
Equating the standardised value of $42$ with $z_2$ gives the second simultaneous equation.
Subtracting the two ‘value $-\ \mu = z\sigma$’ forms cancels $\mu$; the $12$ cm gap spans about $2.12$ standard deviations.
Dividing the length gap by the standardised gap gives a standard deviation of about $5.65$ cm.
From the first equation $\mu = 30 + 1.2816\,\sigma$. The mean length of about $37.2$ cm sits between the two quoted lengths, as expected.
To three significant figures the model is $X \sim N(37.2,\ 5.65^2)$ cm, answering part (a).

steps.description: Set up part (b) and standardise both limits
steps.explanation: Class 1 covers lengths between $33$ cm and $40$ cm, so we standardise each limit with the parameters just found to obtain a standard-normal interval.
The lower limit $33$ is below the mean, giving a negative $z$, while $40$ is above the mean, giving a positive $z$.

steps.description: Write the probability as a difference of Phi values
steps.explanation: The area between two points equals the cumulative probability up to the upper limit minus that up to the lower limit.
So about $0.461$ of the area lies in the Class 1 band.
About $46.1\%$ of cucumbers are graded Class 1. This is a little under half, which is reasonable since the band $33$–$40$ cm sits around the mean but is narrower than the full spread of lengths.
